posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Great nose! Sandalwood, plant matter, cherry and raspberry. Flesh with detail in the mouth. So bright and clear. Crackles with energy on the finish. Super balance.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Love when my Beaujolais “crackles”!!!

Thanks for the note, I’m hearing nice things on 2020 but haven’t tried anything yet.

posts.name: matthias homann
posts.cooked:
I tried the 2020 Delys VV 1926 last week.

First, strong and very typical Morgon fruit flavors, indeed bright and clear, almost transparent.
Then developing quite some intensity with a mix of fruity acids, some tannin and alcohol (14%).
Wouldn’t say out of balance, but - being so young - a tad aggressive; will surely flesh out and mellow within a few years.

I very much enjoyed the 2019 Corcelette VV Sable and actually preferred the package of the '19 Sable to the '20 Delys 1926 with all the ingredients in better balance.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I tried the 2020 Delys VV 1926 last week.

First, strong and very typical Morgon fruit flavors, indeed bright and clear, almost transparent.
Then developing quite some intensity with a mix of fruity acids, some tannin and alcohol (14%).
Wouldn’t say out of balance, but - being so young - a tad aggressive; will surely flesh out and mellow within a few years.

I very much enjoyed the 2019 Corcelette VV Sable and actually preferred the package of the '19 Sable to the '20 Delys 1926 with all the ingredients in better balance.

I found the 2018 Delys a bit much as well, definitely pushes 14%+. Have you tried 2019?

posts.name: matthias homann
posts.cooked:
Yes i did, but I have to admit that the label just said “Delys” without the “1926” addition, while my retailer convincingly argued it actually was the 1926 (Bouland running out of the correct labels).

So, tastewise, the 2019 Delys was sooooo delicious and easy to drink, no alcohol or aggressiveness at all.
the bottle was the first to be emptied, before the Sable.
The Sable however, was more concentrated, more complex, longer, simply better.

I know, this won’t help much - but I liked both 2019 very much and bought quite a lot.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Yes i did, but I have to admit that the label just said “Delys” without the “1926” addition, while my retailer convincingly argued it actually was the 1926 (Bouland running out of the correct labels).

So, tastewise, the 2019 Delys was sooooo delicious and easy to drink, no alcohol or aggressiveness at all.
the bottle was the first to be emptied, before the Sable.
The Sable however, was more concentrated, more complex, longer, simply better.

I know, this won’t help much - but I liked both 2019 very much and bought quite a lot.

Just when we thought it couldn’t get any more complicated…

The old vine Delys bottling is definitely the most concentrated wine in the range, though. But it does carry a lot of puppy fat in 2019, so perhaps the structure and underlying depth wasn’t so readily apparent.

Wouldn’t say out of balance, but - being so young - a tad aggressive; will surely flesh out and mellow within a few years.

I very much enjoyed the 2019 Corcelette VV Sable and actually preferred the package of the '19 Sable to the '20 Delys 1926 with all the ingredients in better balance.

I found the 2018 Delys a bit much as well, definitely pushes 14%+. Have you tried 2019?

Did you check in on the 2018 lately?

posts.name: D@vid K@ntr0witz
posts.cooked: I find just about all the Bouland wines to be a bit much for me. I want to like them more than I do.

posts.name: JT Ng
posts.cooked:
Yes i did, but I have to admit that the label just said “Delys” without the “1926” addition, while my retailer convincingly argued it actually was the 1926 (Bouland running out of the correct labels).

So, tastewise, the 2019 Delys was sooooo delicious and easy to drink, no alcohol or aggressiveness at all.
the bottle was the first to be emptied, before the Sable.
The Sable however, was more concentrated, more complex, longer, simply better.

I know, this won’t help much - but I liked both 2019 very much and bought quite a lot.

That sounds more like the basic Les Delys though. I’ve had both and the basic one was basically ready PnP while the 1926 drank best after a few days. Intoxicating aromatics.

posts.name: JT Ng
posts.cooked:
I tried the 2020 Delys VV 1926 last week.

First, strong and very typical Morgon fruit flavors, indeed bright and clear, almost transparent.
Then developing quite some intensity with a mix of fruity acids, some tannin and alcohol (14%).
Wouldn’t say out of balance, but - being so young - a tad aggressive; will surely flesh out and mellow within a few years.

I very much enjoyed the 2019 Corcelette VV Sable and actually preferred the package of the '19 Sable to the '20 Delys 1926 with all the ingredients in better balance.

Give the 2020 a few more months before trying again. I find drinking less than 6 months after bottling to not be a good representative for most Beaujolais (except Nouveau of course).

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: I’ve got the 2020 Sable in my glass now. It is impressive, with an intense core of fruit and more depth than the standard bottling. However, I’d rather drink the standard bottling now as a youngster, for its vibrancy and elegance.

suggested_topics.excerpt:
Opened at a restaurant. Cork in really great condition. A bit surprised how good it looked - like a new bottle. 
Medium garnet. A bit reserved at first, but really opened up after about hour and kept improving with time.…
